items.title: Generalizability of choice architecture interventions
items.author: Susan Michie
items.content: Choice architecture interventions (or ‘nudges’) aim to guide behaviour by changing the proximal physical, social or psychological environment. In this Review, Szaszi and colleagues show that the average effectiveness of these interventions is small and variable and outline how researchers can gather evidence defining this generalizability more efficiently.

items.title: Bridging qualitative methods and open research
items.author: Madeleine Pownall
items.content: Open research in psychology has advanced transparency and accountability but remains dominated by quantitative, positivist frameworks. For open research to be truly transformative, it must embrace methodological pluralism, learn from qualitative traditions and accommodate all research approaches.

items.title: More lived experience research is needed in Asia
items.author: Jonathan Han Loong Kuek
items.content: Although Western mental health research has started incorporating lived experience expertise, this approach has not been widely adopted in most Asian countries. Embracing the unique perspectives of Asian people with lived experience can provide a more nuanced understanding of mental health and recovery.

items.title: The need for context in intergroup conflict research
items.author: Rezarta Bilali
items.content: Research into intergroup conflict often overlooks the sociopolitical and historical contexts that shape psychological processes. This lack of attention to context can lead to inaccurate understanding of drivers of conflict and ineffective or harmful strategies to address it.

items.title: Menopause as a biological and psychological transition
items.author: Carolyn J. Gibson
items.content: There are complex interactions between physical changes during the menopause transition and mental health and wellbeing. In this Review, Thurston et al. integrate these interactions and outline behavioural approaches to managing menopausal symptoms and related mental health concerns.
